perturbation.original_text: Any payment due pursuant to this Section that is not received by SDBF within fifteen (15) days after such payment is due shall bear interest from the date the payment was due until paid, at a per annum rate of interest equal to the prime rate of interest as published in the Wall Street Journal, plus five (5) percentage points, or if such interest rate is not permitted by applicable law, at the highest non-usurious interest rate permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any payment due pursuant to this Section that is not received by SDBF within five (5) days after such payment is due shall bear interest from the date the payment was due until paid, at a per annum rate of interest equal to the prime rate of interest as published in the Wall Street Journal, plus fifteen (15) percentage points, or if such interest rate is not permitted by applicable law, at the highest non-usurious interest rate permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: This change reduces the grace period for late payments to 5 days and significantly increases the interest rate. The increased interest rate might violate California usury laws depending on the nature of the transaction and the parties involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits the interest rate for loans and forbearances.  While there are exemptions (e.g., for licensed lenders), a 15% above prime interest rate, especially if the prime rate is high, could exceed the legal limit if none of the exemptions apply. Lowering the grace period could compound this issue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the interest rate to prime plus 15%, which, according to the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, might violate California usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the interest rate upon the loan or forbearance of any money shall be 7 percent per annum, unless it falls under exceptions allowing higher rates. The snippet mentions specific limits (10% for personal/family/household purposes or a floating rate for other uses). The change introduces a potentially usurious rate, thus contradicting California Constitution Article XV Section 1.

perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall not claim prevention of or interference with performance of Sponsor's obligations pursuant to this Agreement or the suspension or termination of Sponsor's obligations under this Agreement as the result of any default under this Agreement, without first giving a copy of any notice of default or termination to the Collateral Trustee and providing the Collateral Trustee with the opportunity to cure any such default within one hundred twenty (120) days after the date of such notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall not claim prevention of or interference with performance of Sponsor's obligations pursuant to this Agreement or the suspension or termination of Sponsor's obligations under this Agreement as the result of any default under this Agreement, without first giving a copy of any notice of default or termination to the Collateral Trustee and providing the Collateral Trustee with the opportunity to cure any such default within thirty (30) days after the date of such notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change reduces the cure period for defaults to 30 days. If the underlying debt secured by the collateral has consumer protections that mandate longer cure periods (e.g., under California Foreclosure laws if the sponsorship was somehow collateral for a consumer debt), this shortened period would be unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Homeowner Bill of Rights (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 2920.5, 2923.5, et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement isn't directly a mortgage or deed of trust, if the collateral trustee's rights are derived from a secured loan that is subject to California's Homeowner Bill of Rights (HBOR), a 30-day cure period may be insufficient. HBOR provides specific notice requirements and timelines for foreclosure, which this 30-day cure period could potentially circumvent if the original obligation involved a dwelling.

PERSONS [ 38 - 86 ] DIVISION 2. PROPERTY [ 654 - 1422 ] DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [ 1427 - 3273.69 ] DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [ 3274 - 9566 ]
perturbation.justification: The change from 120 days to 30 days to cure a default contradicts potential consumer protection laws like California's Homeowner Bill of Rights (HBOR) if the collateral secures a loan subject to those protections. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that a shorter cure period may violate these laws if they mandate longer periods. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is broad, it establishes that California Civil Code (CIV) governs obligations, implying that specific provisions within CIV, such as those related to foreclosure, could be violated by the reduced cure period.
